message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it; for a code change the named regression test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if the fix were reverted). Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix G-007 in scripts/capture_transcripts.py
What: transcript scrubber redacts the bare operator username everywhere (derived from home dir), not only in /Users|/home paths.
Test(s): test_transcript_scrub_redacts_bare_operator_username
Steps: read the actual changed file(s); read the test and judge whether it discriminates; check no regression. For a doc/refactor item, judge correctness/coverage rather than revert-discrimination. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested). Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED] _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
56	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
57	
58	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
59	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
60	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
61	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
62	    """
63	    try:
64	        name = Path.home().name
65	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
66	        return None
67	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
68	        return None
69	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
70	
71	
72	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
73	
74	
75	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
76	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
77	        if pat.groups == 2:
78	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
79	        elif pat.groups == 1:
80	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
81	        else:
82	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
83	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
84	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
85	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
86	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
87	    return text
88	
89	
90	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
91	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
92	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
93	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
94	
95	
96	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
97	    try:
98	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
99	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
100	        if srcf.suffix == ".jsonl":
101	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
102	        else:
103	            data = raw
104	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
105	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
106	        else:
107	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
108	        return {
109	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
110	            "bytes": len(data),
111	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
112	        }
113	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
114	        return None
115	
116	
117	def main() -> int:
118	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
119	    if not src.is_dir():
120	        return 0
121	    session = _active_session(src)
122	    if session is None:
123	        return 0
124	    sid = session.stem
125	    captured: list[dict] = []
126	
127	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
128	    if rec:
129	        captured.append(rec)
130	
131	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
132	    subtree = src / sid
133	    if subtree.is_dir():
134	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
135	            if f.is_file():
136	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
137	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
138	                if rec:
139	                    captured.append(rec)
140	
141	    try:
142	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
143	        MANIFEST.write_text(
144	            json.dumps(
145	                {
146	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
147	                    "active_session": sid,
148	                    "n_files": len(captured),
149	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
150	                    "files": captured,
151	                },
152	                indent=2,
153	            )
154	            + "\n",
155	            encoding="utf-8",
156	        )
157	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
158	        return 0
159	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
160	    return 0
161	
162	
163	if __name__ == "__main__":
164	    sys.exit(main())
165

1	"""Guards for repo scripts + examples (pass 3: #F-051, #F-048).
2	
3	These exercise non-package files (scripts/, examples/) that the wheel never ships but that run
4	on every commit (transcript capture) or are copied by users (the example). Where a test targets
5	a file's *content* it reads THAT file and asserts both the corrected form and the absence of the
6	stale/insecure form (the artifact-vs-test fidelity rule).
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import importlib.util
12	from pathlib import Path
13	
14	_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
15	
16	
17	def _load(rel: str, name: str):
18	    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(name, _ROOT / rel)
19	    assert spec and spec.loader
20	    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
21	    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
22	    return mod
23	
24	
25	def test_bearer_token_scrub_covers_base64_chars():
26	    # #F-051: a bearer token containing base64 chars (+ / =) must be FULLY redacted, not split at
27	    # the first such char leaving a trailing fragment in the transcript.
28	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts")
29	    text = "Authorization: [REDACTED]"
30	    scrubbed = cap._scrub(text)
31	    assert "[REDACTED]" in scrubbed
32	    # no recognizable token fragment survives
33	    for frag in ("eyJhbGc", "payload/signature", "/signature", "iOiJIUzI1NiJ9"):
34	        assert frag not in scrubbed, f"token fragment {frag!r} leaked past redaction"
35	
36	
37	def test_license_gate_closes_the_report_file(tmp_path):
38	    # #F-050: the report must be opened via a context manager so the fd is closed deterministically.
39	    # Discriminating: intercept the module's open() and capture the file handle; after main()
40	    # returns, assert it is .closed. A bare json.load(open(...)) leaves the handle open (closed
41	    # only later by GC), so this fails when the context-manager fix is reverted.
42	    import builtins
43	    import json
44	
45	    gate = _load("scripts/license_gate.py", "license_gate_fd")
46	    p = tmp_path / "licenses.json"
47	    p.write_text(json.dumps([{"Name": "ok", "Version": "1", "License": "MIT"}]), encoding="utf-8")
48	
49	    handles = []
50	    real_open = builtins.open
51	
52	    def tracking_open(*a, **k):
53	        fh = real_open(*a, **k)
54	        handles.append(fh)
55	        return fh
56	
57	    gate.open = tracking_open  # module globals win over builtins for the bare open() call
58	    rc = gate.main(str(p))
59	    assert rc == 0
60	    assert handles and all(h.closed for h in handles), (
61	        "license_gate left the report file open (#F-050)"
62	    )
63	
64	
65	def test_transcript_scrub_redacts_operator_pii():
66	    # #F-024 (mandate-compatible mitigation): transcripts are committed as provenance per the
67	    # campaign mandate, but the capture scrubber must mask operator PII — email addresses and the
68	    # home-path username — so identity is not published. The path STRUCTURE is preserved.
69	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_pii")
70	    out = cap._scrub("user [REDACTED] at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/x.py and /home/[REDACTED]/y")
71	    assert "[REDACTED]" not in out
72	    assert "somebody" not in out
73	    assert "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/x.py" in out  # structure kept, username masked
74	    assert "/home/[REDACTED]/y" in out
75	
76	
77	def test_transcript_scrub_redacts_bare_operator_username():
78	    # #G-007: the operator username must be redacted wherever it appears (git author strings,
79	    # project-dir slugs like -Users-[REDACTED]-..., prose), not only inside /Users|/home paths.
80	    from pathlib import Path
81	
82	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_user")
83	    name = Path.home().name
84	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
85	        import pytest
86	
87	        pytest.skip("home username too short to redact safely")
88	    text = f"git author: {name} <x@y.z>; project dir -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
89	    out = cap._scrub(text)
90	    assert name not in out  # bare handle masked everywhere, not only in a /Users path
91	
92	
93	def test_example_uses_no_hardcoded_hmac_secret():
94	    # #F-048: the example must not carry a shippable, publicly-known HMAC secret a user could copy
95	    # into production. It derives a fresh random secret per run instead.
96	    src = (_ROOT / "examples" / "minimal_study.py").read_text(encoding="utf-8")
97	    assert b"example-study-secret-key".decode() not in src  # the stale weak literal is gone
98	    assert "os.urandom(32)" in src  # the fresh-per-run secret is in place
99

content.text:
Now let me analyze. The fix derives the username from the home dir and redacts it everywhere. Let me verify the test discriminates and check for regressions.

Key consideration: does the test discriminate (fail if fix reverted)? Let me check what "reverting" looks like — removing the `_USERNAME_RE` block. The test uses `name = Path.home().name`. In the test environment, what is the home name?

content.text:
The test discriminates: it fails when the fix is reverted, and the existing PII test still passes (no regression in that interaction). File restored cleanly.

Now let me consider correctness/regression concerns of the fix itself:

1. **Username derived from home dir** — `Path.home().name` = "[REDACTED]". Redacted everywhere as a bare token via `re.escape`.

2. **Ordering**: username redaction runs *after* path rule. Good — but note the path rule `(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s"']+` already masks `/Users/[REDACTED] → `/Users/[REDACTED] The username pass then catches the slug `-Users-[REDACTED]-` and git authors. Correct.

3. **Potential over-redaction / substring concern**: `re.escape(name)` with no word boundaries. If username is a substring of an unrelated word, it gets redacted. The guard `len(name) < 4` mitigates trivial cases but "[REDACTED]" (11 chars) is unlikely to collide. The docstring acknowledges this tradeoff. For a PII-scrubber, over-redaction is the safe direction. Acceptable.

4. **`pat.groups == 2` / `== 1`**: `pat.groups` is an int attribute, compared correctly. Pre-existing, not part of this fix.

Let me confirm the full test suite isn't broken and check the email pattern ordering doesn't conflict.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python - <<'EOF'
import importlib.util,pathlib
spec=importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("ct","scripts/capture_transcripts.py")
m=importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec);spec.loader.exec_module(m)
# regression: a short common word should NOT be over-redacted if it equals nothing; check normal text untouched
print(repr(m._scrub("the quick brown fox")))
# username inside email local part case
print(repr(m._scrub("contact [REDACTED]")))
EOF

content.text:
Benign text untouched; email handled by the email pattern first. The fix is appropriate and the test discriminates with no regression.

Summary:
- File: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py` — adds `_username_pattern()` deriving the handle from `Path.home().name`, guards against short/None handles (`len < 4`), and applies `_USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)` last in `_scrub()` (after the path rule).
- Test: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_scripts.py::test_transcript_scrub_redacts_bare_operator_username` asserts the bare handle is gone from git-author and project-slug forms; skips safely if home name < 4 chars.
- Discrimination confirmed: reverting the `_USERNAME_RE` block fails the test (`'[REDACTED]' is contained here: -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc`); restored cleanly.
- No regression: existing PII test still passes; benign text and email-local-part collisions handled correctly; over-redaction risk is the safe direction for a PII scrubber and is documented.

input.reasoning:
The fix in scripts/capture_transcripts.py adds _username_pattern() which derives the operator handle from Path.home().name and, in _scrub(), redacts that bare handle everywhere via _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text). It runs LAST (after the /Users|/home path rule), so it additionally catches the handle in git-author strings, prose, and the project-dir slug (-Users-[REDACTED]-...), exactly the leak G-007 describes. It guards against None/short (<4 char) handles to avoid over-redacting common words, a sensible safety check documented in the docstring.

Appropriate: idiomatic (re.escape, compiled at import), correct ordering, fails safe (returns None on missing home, never breaks capture). No regression observed — the sibling PII test (test_transcript_scrub_redacts_operator_pii) still passes, path STRUCTURE is preserved (/Users/[REDACTED]/...), benign text "the quick brown fox" is untouched, and an email containing the username is handled by the earlier email pattern. Residual over-redaction risk (substring match, no word boundary) is the safe direction for a PII scrubber and is acknowledged.

Complete + DISCRIMINATING: test_transcript_scrub_redacts_bare_operator_username asserts the bare handle is absent from a git-author + slug string (and skips safely when the home name is <4 chars). I verified discrimination by removing the _USERNAME_RE block (revert simulation): the test FAILS with "'[REDACTED]' is contained here: -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc", while it PASSES with the fix in place. File restored; full tests/test_scripts.py is 5 passed. Both appropriate and complete.

content.text: Verdict: CONFIRMED. The G-007 fix is both appropriate and complete, and the named regression test discriminates (fails on revert, passes with the fix).
